message:
feat(runtime,doctor): add CI artifact contract, rollout drills, and traceability matrix tests

- tests/ci_artifact_contract.rs: new test verifying CI artifact integrity
- tests/rollout_drills.rs: new rollout simulation test scenarios
- tests/traceability_matrix.rs: new requirement-to-test traceability
- doctor/tests/asupersync_validation.rs: new Asupersync validation test
- Expand layout_lab, layout engine, process_subscription, program,
  and simulator modules
- Update lifecycle_contract tests
- Sync beads tracker

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

Commands:
- cargo test -p ftui-demo-showcase --test visual_effects_pty pty_vfx_perf_regression_guard --release -- --nocapture
- cargo test -p ftui-demo-showcase --test visual_effects_pty vfx_golden_hash_registry -- --nocapture
- cargo test -p ftui-demo-showcase --test visual_effects_pty pty_vfx_harness_deterministic_hashes -- --nocapture

Perf guard results (seed=0, tick=16ms, frames=120):
120x40 total_ms_p95 (baseline -> now, Δ):
- metaballs: 3.609 -> 2.849 (-21.06%)
- plasma:    2.649 -> 2.221 (-16.16%)
- doom-e1m1:  1.666 -> 0.732 (-56.06%)
- quake-e1m1: 5.553 -> 1.885 (-66.05%)
- mandelbrot: 6.946 -> 3.672 (-47.14%)

Golden hash registry: PASS (metaballs/plasma/doom-e1m1/quake-e1m1/mandelbrot @120x40, seed=42, 6 frames)
Deterministic hash check: PASS (metaballs @120x40, seed=42, 6 frames)

Conclusion: ≥30% p95 total reduction achieved for at least two heavy effects (doom-e1m1 + quake-e1m1), so bd-3e1t.5.3 success target looks met. Remaining doc refresh work is tracked in bd-3e1t.5.3.15.","created_at":"2026-02-10T05:47:09Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1cgh9","title":"task(graveyard-§7.5): ART (Adaptive Radix Tree) for command palette search","description":"Use an Adaptive Radix Tree (ART) for the command palette prefix search, providing O(k) lookup where k = key length, independent of dataset size. ART adapts node size to key distribution, achieving near-optimal memory usage.

## Graveyard Entry

Section 7.5 -- Adaptive Radix Tree
Priority: 3

## Background and Math Foundations

ART (Leis et al. 2013, The Adaptive Radix Tree: ARTful Indexing for Main-Memory Databases) is a radix tree with adaptive node sizes:
- Node4: 4 keys, 4 children (for sparse nodes)
- Node16: 16 keys, 16 children (SIMD-searchable)
- Node48: 256-entry index array + 48 children (for denser nodes)
- Node256: 256 children (for very dense nodes)

Performance characteristics:
- Lookup: O(k) where k = key length (NOT dependent on n = number of keys)
- Prefix scan: O(k + m) where m = number of matches (ideal for autocomplete)
- Memory: adapts to key distribution (sparse keys use small nodes)
- Cache performance: nodes fit in cache lines (Node4 = 32 bytes, Node16 = 128 bytes)

For command palette:
- Commands are strings (e.g., file:open, edit:undo, view:toggle-sidebar)
- User types prefix, ART returns all matching commands
- With 1000+ commands, ART provides instant results vs O(n) linear scan
- Fuzzy matching: extend with edit-distance bounded search (Damerau-Levenshtein)

## Structured Logging

All tests and benchmarks must emit structured JSONL evidence:
- op: art_insert, key, node_type_before, node_type_after, depth, timestamp_ns
- op: art_lookup, prefix, match_count, scan_time_ns, node_visits
- op: art_benchmark, command_count, art_time_ns, btree_time_ns, linear_time_ns, speedup
- op: art_memory, command_count, art_bytes, btree_bytes, node_type_distribution

## Acceptance Criteria

1. ART implementation with insert, lookup, prefix_scan, delete
2. Adaptive node types (Node4, Node16, Node48, Node256) with automatic promotion/demotion
3. Prefix scan returns results in sorted order (for display)
4. Benchmark: ART vs BTreeMap vs linear scan at 100, 1K, 10K commands
5. Integration with command palette widget
6. Optional: fuzzy matching with bounded edit distance
7. All tests emit structured JSONL logs for evidence-ledger integration
8. CI artifacts: lookup latency comparison, memory usage report, node distribution","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:53.779268148Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:16:25.507245285Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["adaptive-radix","alien-cs","art","command-palette","graveyard","search"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1cgh9","depends_on_id":"bd-14k2m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T16:31:30Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1cgh9","depends_on_id":"bd-3jlw5.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T16:31:30Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

Will implement E2E PTY resize storm tests with verbose JSONL logging, covering resize sequences, tear detection, and reflow correctness.","created_at":"2026-02-03T19:18:12Z"},{"id":282,"issue_id":"bd-1rz0.9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SilentDune completed: Created resize_storm_e2e.rs with 23 E2E tests covering 6 invariants (CONTENT-1, GHOST-1, TEAR-1, DET-1, PIPE-1, BUDGET-1). Tests exercise full render pipeline (Buffer→Diff→Presenter→HeadlessTerm) under burst, sweep, oscillate, pathological, and mixed storm patterns with verbose JSONL logging, flicker analysis integration, and performance budget enforcement.","created_at":"2026-02-03T19:26:50Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1rzr","title":"Action Timeline: Alert Threshold Calibration (Conformal + e-process)","description":"## Goal
Set change-point alert thresholds with formal false-positive control and provide an explanation ledger per alert.

## Core Idea (Alien Artifact)
Conformal thresholding on residuals from the sketch baseline:
- Given calibration residuals R = {r_1..r_n}, threshold q = quantile_{1-alpha} using (n+1) rule.
- Alert when r_t > q.

Anytime-valid layer:
- Maintain an e-process on residuals to allow early alerts without inflating false positives.
- Example: e_t = exp(lambda * (r_t - mu) - (lambda^2 * sigma^2)/2), E_t = product e_t.
- Trigger if E_t > 1/alpha.

## Acceptance Criteria
- FPR <= alpha on synthetic and recorded traces.
- Alert carries evidence ledger: residual, q, conformal score, e-value, alpha.
- No alert spam under steady-state traffic.

## Tests (REQUIRED)
- unit_conformal_threshold: threshold equals (1-alpha) quantile of calibration set.
- unit_evalue_monotone: e-process nonnegative and grows with evidence.
- property_fpr_control: empirical FPR <= alpha + tolerance.

## E2E (REQUIRED)
- Replay trace with known change points; log alert timing and evidence.

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

- Conformal thresholding using (n+1) quantile rule

- 27 passing unit tests including FPR property tests
- GRAPA adaptive lambda support","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-1sder","title":"task(asupersync-architecture): define invariants, user-visible metrics, and canonical evidence schema","description":"Background:
The migration needs explicit invariants and user-visible success metrics. Otherwise validation becomes vague and benchmarks measure the wrong things.

Goal:
Define the invariant set, user/operator metrics, and evidence schema that every later task must trace back to.","design":"Work packages:
- Enumerate correctness invariants: semantic parity, deterministic output, shutdown guarantees, terminal safety, replay identity.
- Enumerate user/operator metrics: effect turnaround, shutdown latency, child-process cancellation latency, failure diagnosability, doctor completion time, artifact completeness.
- Define the canonical evidence schema and correlation IDs that tests and scripts must emit.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Every later test and rollout task can point to named invariants and metrics from this bead.
2. Metrics are user-meaningful and operator-meaningful, not only internal counters.
3. The evidence schema is rich enough to drive replay and go/no-go decisions.","notes":"This bead is the backbone for the validation traceability matrix and benchmark gates.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"AmberBadger","created_at":"2026-03-10T01:17:31.141235394Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-12T11:22:28Z","closed_at":"2026-03-12T11:22:28Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","asupersync-frankentui","evidence","invariants","metrics"]}
 {"id":"bd-1sm6v","title":"epic(alien-artifact): Galaxy-Brain Transparency Demo Screen","description":"Live UI showing: (1) posterior distributions for all active decisions, (2) top evidence terms with Bayes factors, (3) chosen actions with expected loss, (4) confidence intervals. Force drift then show automatic fallback. The 'killer demo' from FrankenSuite summary. This is what makes FrankenTUI feel like an alien artifact — the math is visible and beautiful.

**User Value:** Interactive galaxy-brain cards let users see exactly why each adaptive decision was made, building trust in the framework.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Decision card widget renders at all 4 disclosure levels
- Live sparkline shows posterior probability evolution
- Demo screen works in both inline and alt-screen modes
- All existing tests pass","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:50:12.751206044Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:40.109226266Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:40.109203323Z","close_reason":"Merged into bd-1lgz8 (existing Killer Demo Galaxy-Brain Transparency epic)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-artifact","demo","galaxy-brain"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1sm6v","depends_on_id":"bd-fp38v","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T16:31:31Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
@@ -1004,7 +1004,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-2oovu","title":"Complexity Guards + Degradation Strategy","description":"Background
- Large or pathological graphs can blow up layout/routing cost.
- We need deterministic guardrails and graceful degradation.

Scope
- Define complexity limits: max nodes/edges, route search budget, layout iterations.
- Compute a diagram complexity score (nodes, edges, labels, clusters).
- Degradation modes: hide labels, collapse clusters, simplify routing, reduce decoration, or ASCII fallback.
- Log guard triggers + chosen degradation in JSONL with codes.

Considerations
- Guardrails must be deterministic and based on config.
- Degradation should preserve readability and avoid crashes.

Tests / Validation
- Unit tests for guard triggers at thresholds.
- Snapshot tests showing degraded outputs.
- PTY E2E logs verify guard activation.

Success Criteria
- Engine never crashes on oversized inputs; produces predictable degraded output.","notes":"Plan draft: add MermaidComplexity report (nodes, edges, clusters, labels, ports, link_count, label_chars/lines) + score; compare to MermaidConfig limits (max_nodes/max_edges/max_label_*, route_budget, layout_iteration_budget) and emit warnings + degradation plan (hide labels, collapse clusters, simplify routing, ASCII fallback). Likely store in MermaidDiagramMeta or new MermaidDegradationPlan struct for downstream renderer + JSONL logging.  {"id":"bd-2orqr","title":"Diagram Type: State Diagram (v2)","description":"Background
- State diagrams are core to architecture and control flow docs.

Scope
- Parse stateDiagram-v2 syntax (states, transitions, composite states, start/end nodes).
- Support notes and internal transitions.
- Layout with nested boxes for composite states.
- Route transitions with orthogonal edges and labels.

Considerations
- Nested layout must preserve boundaries and padding.
- Transitions crossing state boundaries should be routed around edges.

Tests / Validation
- Unit tests for nested state parsing/layout.
- Snapshot tests at multiple sizes.
- PTY E2E logs: diagram_type=state, size, tier, warnings.

Success Criteria
- Nested states render without overlap.
- Transitions readable at small sizes.","notes":"Initial audit: stateDiagram-v2 currently parsed via generic graph path in mermaid.rs; no [*], state blocks, or notes parsing yet. Plan: extend parser for start/end nodes ([*]), state declarations (state X / state "Label" as X), composite blocks (state X { ... }), and note lines; map to IR with cluster membership + implicit start/end nodes; update layout to keep nested boundaries; add fixtures/snapshots for nested states + start/end; ensure deterministic ordering + label wrap. Waiting on mermaid.rs/mermaid_layout.rs reservation window (TurquoiseBeacon).

Implementation sketch:
- Add Statement::StateDecl / StateBlockStart / StateBlockEnd / StateNote (single + multiline note...end note).
- Parse in stateDiagram branch before parse_edge/parse_node.
- Start/end: detect `[*]` tokens; map to synthetic ids using span (e.g., __start_L{line}_C{col}) with label "*".
- Composite: create cluster draft with title (label or id). Add hidden node class (e.g., "composite_container") for edge targets; renderer skips drawing nodes with that class.
- Notes: create note nodes with label text; add dashed edge `-.->` to target; class "note" for future styling.

 {"id":"bd-2pip","title":"Add degradation support to complex widgets (Category D)","description":"## Scope

Enhance Category D widgets - complex widgets that could benefit from 
performance-adaptive rendering via the degradation level.

## Frame Features Used

- `frame.degradation` - Current performance budget constraint level

## Degradation Levels

```rust
pub enum DegradationLevel {
    Full,           // All features enabled
    ReducedEffects, // Skip animations, use simpler borders
    EssentialOnly,  // Only render essential content
    Minimal,        // Absolute minimum for usability
}
```

## Migration Pattern

1. Change signature to use Frame
2. Check degradation level for complex rendering:
   ```rust
   fn render(&self, area: Rect, frame: &mut Frame) {
       let buf = &mut frame.buffer;
       
       match frame.degradation {
           DegradationLevel::Full => {
               // Full fancy rendering
               self.render_unicode_borders(buf, area);
               self.render_shadows(buf, area);
           }
           DegradationLevel::ReducedEffects => {
               // Skip shadows, keep unicode borders
               self.render_unicode_borders(buf, area);
           }
           DegradationLevel::EssentialOnly | DegradationLevel::Minimal => {
               // ASCII-only borders
               self.render_ascii_borders(buf, area);
           }
       }
   }
   ```

## Widgets Expected in This Category

Based on complexity (to be confirmed by audit):
- Block (unicode vs ASCII borders)
- Table (skip row highlighting, simpler separators)
- Charts (reduce data points, skip animations)
- Any widget with heavy rendering

## Important

- This is OPTIONAL enhancement, not required for correctness
- Can be done incrementally after basic migration
- Focus on widgets that are actually performance bottlenecks
- Don't over-engineer - only add if there's measurable benefit

## Verification

- `cargo check -p ftui-widgets` must pass
- Benchmark comparison at different degradation levels (if perf test exists)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T23:22:43.861931657Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T23:27:10.024557952Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T23:27:10.024524789Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2pip","depends_on_id":"bd-z8q6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T16:31:31Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":384,"issue_id":"bd-2pip","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Audit Finding: Category D Widgets Already Have Degradation Support

Based on the widget audit, the following widgets already implement degradation:

1. **ProgressBar** (progress.rs):
   - At EssentialOnly: Shows percentage text only, no bar
   - At Skeleton: Skips entirely
   - Uses `frame.buffer.degradation.render_decorative()`

2. **Spinner** (spinner.rs):
   - At EssentialOnly: Shows label only, no animation
   - At Skeleton: Skips entirely  
   - At SimpleBorders: Uses ASCII fallback

3. **Scrollbar** (scrollbar.rs):
   - At EssentialOnly: Skips entirely (decorative)
   - At Skeleton: Skips entirely

4. **Rule** (rule.rs):
   - At EssentialOnly: Skips entirely (decorative)
   - At SimpleBorders: Uses ASCII '-' instead of Unicode '─'

**Recommendation**: This task can be closed as the basic degradation support is already implemented. Further enhancement (Block, Table) can be done incrementally as needed per the task description 'OPTIONAL enhancement'.","created_at":"2026-02-01T23:27:05Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2pkmp","title":"Half-Block Node Interior Fills with Color","description":"## Goal
Use half-block characters (▀▄█) and background colors to render filled node interiors, giving nodes visual weight and shape distinction even in cell-based (non-Canvas) rendering mode.

## Why
- Currently nodes are just borders + labels with no interior fill. They look hollow and blend together.
- A subtle colored fill makes shapes visually distinct: even two rectangles with different fill colors communicate "these are different types."
- Half-block characters give 1×2 vertical resolution for top/bottom edge rendering — fills look smoother.
- Works on any terminal that supports colors (no Braille/Unicode required).

## Implementation
- For each node, after drawing the border, fill the interior cells with the node's theme background color.
- Use █ (full block) for completely filled cells.
- Use ▀ (upper half) and ▄ (lower half) at the top and bottom edges for smoother shape boundaries.
- For diamond/hexagon shapes: compute which cells are "inside" the shape boundary and fill only those.
- Fill color comes from DiagramPalette (idea #7) or style_refs in the IR.

## Integration with Shape Rendering
- Must work with cell-mode shape rendering (idea #2): fill happens AFTER border drawing, BEFORE label writing.
- Label cells override fill (text on top of colored background).

## Files
- crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid_render.rs (add fill_node_interior method)

## Tests
- Unit: filled rectangle has correct cells
- Snapshot: nodes with various fill colors at 80×24
- Verify label text remains readable on filled background (contrast check)","notes":"Implemented in commit 48c04486. Per-node fill background colors via DiagramPalette::node_fill_for(). Half-block smoothing (▄▀) for Rect/Rounded/Circle shapes in Unicode mode. 4 new tests: node_fill_has_background_color, node_fill_half_block_top_row, node_fill_ascii_mode_no_half_blocks, node_label_preserves_fill_background. All 2282 ftui-extras tests pass, clippy clean.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CopperHawk","created_at":"2026-02-05T15:29:48.751593031Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T18:13:07.661012848Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T18:13:07.660930504Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mermaid","rendering","visual-polish"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2pkmp","depends_on_id":"bd-1emsq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T16:31:31Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2pkmp","depends_on_id":"bd-1qkni","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T16:31:31Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":385,"issue_id":"bd-2pkmp","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"GoldBear claiming. Will implement half-block node interior fills using ▀▄█ characters with theme background colors.","created_at":"2026-02-05T18:43:59Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-cn7eq","title":"task(perf-rollout): define promotion scorecards and go/no-go gates for keeping optimization changes","description":"Background:\nEventually the project needs an explicit rule for deciding whether a performance change is safe and valuable enough to keep. That rule should synthesize baseline deltas, hotspot movement, proof artifacts, gauntlet results, tail monitors, and operator-facing evidence quality instead of letting each reviewer make up their own standard.\n\nGoal:\nCreate the canonical scorecard and promotion policy for optimization work.","design":"Work packages:
- Define the required evidence categories for acceptance, including baseline deltas, proof success, validation-matrix completeness, gauntlet results, tail behavior, rollback readiness, and user-visible benchmark outcomes.
- Specify hard blockers versus softer concerns that can be carried as residual risk.
- Explain how reviewers should reason about tradeoffs such as modest average win versus strong tail improvement.
- Record the minimum evidence needed for local adoption, CI acceptance, and broader default enablement if relevant.
- Ensure the scorecard speaks the same language as the evidence ledger so verdicts are traceable.
- Treat this bead as a policy-definition step that can be completed before every gauntlet is implemented; later gauntlet beads should then produce evidence against this scorecard instead of forcing reviewers to invent standards after the fact.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Different reviewers applying this bead should reach roughly the same go/no-go conclusion for the same evidence set.
2. The bead makes optimization acceptance a policy question rather than a vibes question.
3. Future contributors can use the scorecard without consulting any external planning notes.
4. A change cannot score as promotable if required unit/property/integration/E2E/replay evidence or detailed logging artifacts are missing.
5. A change also cannot score as broadly promotable if its wins disappear on challenge fixtures or if negative-control scenarios reveal fragile behavior that the canonical suites missed.","notes":"Planning correction: the scorecard now explicitly guards against overfitting to narrow benchmark suites. Promotion requires robustness evidence, not just strong numbers on curated workloads. Additional refinement: we should not promote changes that are fast but operationally opaque to debug when they fail.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-14T18:46:37.596417722Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-15T01:40:58.836429421Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-artifact-coding","ci","extreme-software-optimization","ftui-performance-control-plane","performance","performance-rollout","scorecard","validation"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-cn7eq","depends_on_id":"bd-1vb19","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-14T19:09:28.958920905Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-cn7eq","depends_on_id":"bd-8vstx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-14T19:06:30.586463631Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-cn7eq","depends_on_id":"bd-pou7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-14T19:06:12.506822661Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-cn7eq","depends_on_id":"bd-rw97d","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-14T19:01:09.763216958Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-cn7eq","depends_on_id":"bd-zzfhe","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-14T19:01:12.963054005Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-cpwfc","title":"feature(perf-rollout): tail-risk monitoring, CI gates, and safe optimization rollout","description":"Background:\nEven correct optimization work can become dangerous if the project lacks a shared artifact contract, tail-risk monitors, or a go/no-go policy for broader adoption. The rollout layer converts one-off measurements into an operational envelope that future contributors and operators can trust.\n\nGoal:\nCreate the evidence ledger, drift/tail-risk monitors, scorecards, and rollout drills needed to promote performance changes safely.","design":"Feature scope:
- Define a unified artifact contract for baselines, profiles, replays, gauntlets, and validation-matrix outputs.
- Monitor tail regressions and regime shifts instead of only average performance.
- Define promotion gates and scorecards for accepting optimization changes, including validation completeness and logging quality.
- Script shadow, canary, fallback, and rollback drills for performance-sensitive lanes.

Intent:
Optimization work should graduate through evidence, not charisma.","acceptance_criteria":"1. CI and human reviewers consume the same artifact and validation contract.
2. Tail-latency and drift regressions become visible and actionable before broader enablement.
3. The project gains a repeatable way to decide when an optimization is safe enough to keep.
4.
